summary: McKinney applies Fred Brooks's classic 'Mythical Man-Month' framework to the era of AI coding agents, arguing that while agents dramatically reduce accidental complexity, they leave essential complexity untouched and may even make it harder to manage. He describes an 'agentic tar pit' where parallel agent sessions generate code bloat and technical debt at machine speed. The piece warns that near-zero cost of code generation creates dangerous scope creep, both in personal projects and open source. McKinney concludes that design taste, product scoping, and conceptual integrity are now the binding constraints on software quality, not coding speed. He acknowledges agents have transformed his productivity but insists expert human judgment remains indispensable for complex systems. The post is both a practical reflection on his own agentic workflow and a broader argument that Brooks's fifty-year-old insights still hold.
key_points: AI agents excel at reducing accidental complexity but cannot reliably distinguish it from essential complexity, often generating new accidental complexity in the process
Brooks's Law and the coordination problem don't disappear with agents — they change shape, as contradictory agent outputs require human reconciliation
Near-zero code generation cost creates 'agentic scope creep' where every feature is cheap to create but expensive to maintain, test, and reason about
Agent-generated codebases accrue technical debt at unprecedented speed, reaching bloat levels that cause agents themselves to choke on context
Design talent and good taste are now the scarcest resources — the bottleneck was never hands on keyboards
money_quotes: Agents can't reliably tell the difference between essential and accidental complexity.
Agents are so good at attacking accidental complexity, that they generate new accidental complexity that can get in the way of the essential structure that you are trying to build.
When generating code is free, knowing when to say 'no' is your last defense.
This is technical debt on an unprecedented scale, accrued at machine speed.
The bottleneck was never hands on keyboards.
Figuring out what to build was the hard part long before we had LLMs, and I don't see how a flawless coding agent changes that.
The developers who thrive in this new agentic era won't be the ones who run the most parallel sessions or burn the most tokens.
When the person submitting a pull request didn't write or fully read the code in it, there's likely no one involved who's truly accountable for the design decisions.
key_insight: AI coding agents have eliminated the accidental complexity bottleneck, revealing that design judgment, taste, and knowing what not to build were always the true constraints on great software.
